label: ai
topic: Overcoming fear of public speaking to become student body president
text:
The cold, heavy metal of the microphone felt like a block of ice slipping through my sweating palms. I was twelve years old, standing on the polished linoleum of the middle school cafeteria stage. A sea of blurry faces stared back at me, their silence loud and expectant. I opened my mouth to read the opening lines of my student council speech, but my throat closed. The microphone picked up my ragged, amplified breathing, culminating in a high-pitched squeal of audio feedback. I dropped the mic. It hit the floor with a deafening thud, and I fled the stage. For years, that sharp, metallic echo haunted me.

Public speaking wasn't just a fear; it was a physical barricade. In the classroom, I was the girl who mentally counted the desks to figure out which paragraph I would have to read aloud, silently mouthing the words until my turn arrived, my heart hammering against my ribs. I had ideas—I wanted to debate the merits of environmental policies in civics and propose new fundraising ideas for the art club—but the memory of that fallen microphone kept my voice locked in my chest.

My turning point arrived quietly in my sophomore year. My history teacher, Mr. Hayes, noticed I was writing detailed, passionate arguments in my essays but never speaking during class discussions. Instead of forcing me to the front of the room, he asked me to read the morning announcements over the school intercom. "No faces," he promised. "Just a microphone in an empty room."

The first morning, sitting in the dusty AV closet surrounded by the smell of ozone and old electronics, my hands shook. I pressed the gray plastic button. "Good morning, Eagles," I whispered. My voice cracked, but no one laughed. I was hidden. Week by week, the shaking subsided. I began to play with the cadence of my voice, adding inflections and even a brief, dry joke about the cafeteria's mystery meat. The microphone, once a weapon, was becoming a tool.

By junior year, I took a terrifying leap and joined the debate team. The podium felt like an island, but when I gripped the edges of the wood, I grounded myself. I focused on the rough grain beneath my fingertips and the weight of the research cards in my hands. I learned that bravery wasn't the absence of a pounding heart; it was speaking through the rhythm of it.

When senior year arrived, I decided to run for student body president. Standing behind the curtain in the auditorium, listening to the murmurs of five hundred students, the familiar ghost of twelve-year-old me whispered in my ear. My palms began to sweat. But as my name was called, I stepped into the blinding warmth of the stage lights. I didn't reach for the microphone stand. Instead, I unclipped the mic, holding the cool metal firmly in my hand. I looked at the sea of faces, took a deep breath, and began to speak. I didn't drop it this time. I used it to finally share the voice I had spent four years finding.
